topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The realtor’s enthusiasm felt like a physical weight pressing down on Eleanor. She kept smoothing the fabric of her linen trousers, her gaze darting from the antique mahogany mantelpiece to the scuffed patch of wallpaper near the hallway. Every creak of the old floorboards sounded amplified in the sudden silence of the empty house. When she touched the dusty surface of a forgotten picture frame, a tremor ran up her arm, making her pull her hand back quickly. "It's just an old house, Mrs. Albright," the agent chirped, his smile too bright, too fast. Eleanor managed a tight nod, her throat feeling constricted. She kept checking the windows, half-expecting to see movement in the shadows behind the lace curtains. A cold prickle traced its way down her spine, and she silently urged the agent to hurry up and let her out.

[story 2]

Mark traced the outline of a faint discoloration on the baseboard with the tip of his finger. The air in the study was thick, stagnant, carrying a faint, metallic scent that made his stomach clench. He kept glancing toward the darkened doorway, his breath catching shallowly in his chest. It wasn't just the decay of age; there was something else, an unsettling stillness clinging to the heavy velvet drapes. He shifted his weight from one foot to the other, his shoes squeaking faintly on the hardwood. "Nothing's wrong," he muttered to himself, the sound of his own voice sounding thin and reedy. He forced himself to move toward the kitchen, needing the bright, open space, but every shadow seemed to stretch and deepen, watching him from the corners of the room.

[story 3]

When the solicitor showed him the preliminary inspection report, David felt a distinct tightness around his jaw. He reread the section detailing the structural anomalies, his eyes scanning the dense legal jargon as if searching for a hidden warning. The silence of the house, usually comforting, now felt oppressive, heavy with unspoken history. He stood by the bay window, the glass cool against his forehead, and watched the overgrown garden. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped him. He turned slowly, his gaze sweeping over the darkened entryway, his hands clenching into tight fists at his sides. He wanted to bolt, to find solid ground, but his feet felt rooted to the spot, unwilling to move from the unsettling reality the house had revealed.
